interactions.summary: Jiang reads the serpent scene as a debate in which Eve judges for herself, eats first, becomes the real hero, and Adam follows before blaming her.
Jiang interprets Yahweh's response as revolutionary forgiveness and self-reflection, then reframes expulsion and death as conditions for human and species-level creativity.

speaker_notes.note: Jiang moves between quotation, paraphrase, and interpretation; no separate audience speaker occurs in these focus refs.

claims.claim: Eve is the hero of the Eden story because she independently judges the fruit and chooses knowledge before Adam follows.
Yahweh differs from Greek-style vengeful gods because he does not kill Adam and Eve but gives them clothing, implying forgiveness and possible self-reflection.
claims.temporal_scope: Comparative religious interpretation in this lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang interprets the naming of Eve as honoring her as the mother of all living and even as a true goddess figure.
Creativity requires death; without mortality, humans lack urgency and later generations cannot have their own creative life.

signature_moments.moment: Eve looks at the fruit and says, in effect, I can see for myself what is true.
signature_moments.source_phrase: no i can see for myself what is true
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes the fall into the birth of independent judgment.

signature_moments.moment: God's first response to disobedience is a present.
signature_moments.source_phrase: instead of being angry... he offers them a present
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It crystallizes Jiang's Yahweh as forgiveness rather than vengeance.
It is the sharpest formulation of the lecture's mortality-creativity model.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Yahweh as a deity who can recognize his role in a mistake, forgive, and revise his response.
The idea that finite life creates urgency and generational turnover, making creativity possible.
